threats.title: Permanent Denial of Service in buyShares via Zero Reserve State
threats.hypothesis_description: If either the base or quote reserve reaches exactly zero while totalSupply > 0, the buyShares function falls through its conditional branches and attempts to mint 0 shares, which reverts permanently.
threats.investigation_rationale: The if-else logic in buyShares does not handle the edge case where totalSupply > 0 but one reserve is 0. This state can be reached via extreme trading rounding or admin setParameters, permanently bricking new deposits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: falls through if/else branches when totalSupply > 0 and baseReserve == 0, leaving shares = 0

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Incomplete conditional coverage for state variables
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No fallback logic for single-sided zero reserve with existing supply

analysis_questions: Can trading rounding errors naturally drive a reserve to exactly 0?
Can an attacker intentionally drain a reserve to 0 using sellShares?
validation_requirements: Test buyShares behavior when baseReserve == 0 and totalSupply > 0
Attempt to reach baseReserve == 0 via normal protocol interactions

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker or admin drives one reserve to 0. Subsequent users attempting to add liquidity via buyShares will always face a revert, halting pool growth.
prerequisites: Ability to drive one reserve to exactly 0

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of future fee revenue and pool utility
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires admin to call ratioSync after donating tokens, or setParameters

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing edge case test for buyShares with zero reserve and non-zero supply

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Depends on pool liquidity to drain reserve
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily detectable via reverted buyShares transactions

threats.title: Router Pool Creation Blocked for High-Decimal Base Tokens due to Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: The _validateDecimals function uses unsigned subtraction (quoteDecimals - baseDecimals) which reverts if the base token has more decimals than the quote token.
threats.investigation_rationale: Solidity 0.8+ reverts on underflow. This simple math error prevents the Router from creating valid pools (e.g., WETH 18 decimals / USDC 6 decimals) if WETH is set as the base token.

proof_of_signal.pattern: quoteDecimals - baseDecimals > MAX_BASE_QUOTE_DECIMALS_DIFFERENCE without checking which is larger

preconditions: User attempts to create a pool via Router

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Reverts with panic code 0x11 instead of custom error

analysis_questions: Does this completely block creation of common pairs like WETH/USDC?
Can users bypass this by calling Factory directly?
validation_requirements: Deploy Router and attempt to create a pool with 18 decimal base and 6 decimal quote

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user tries to create a WETH/USDC pool via the Router. The transaction reverts due to underflow, forcing the user to either reverse the pair order (changing pricing math) or abandon the creation.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: None directly, but severe UX degradation and protocol friction
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Users must bypass Router and use Factory directly

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for pool creation with baseDecimals > quoteDecimals

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as failed transactions with panic 0x11

threats.title: Fee Evasion in flashLoan via Equal or Greater Balance Returns
threats.hypothesis_description: If a flash loan borrower returns enough tokens such that both base and quote balances are >= their respective reserves, the fee calculation blocks are skipped entirely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The flashLoan function uses if-blocks to detect which token was borrowed. If the borrower returns excess of both tokens, neither block executes, bypassing the mtFee transfer and allowing a free flash loan.

proof_of_signal.pattern: skips fee logic if baseBalance >= _BASE_RESERVE_ and quoteBalance >= _QUOTE_RESERVE_

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No default fee charged if balances are perfectly restored
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing mandatory fee calculation outside of the swap detection blocks

analysis_questions: Can an attacker borrow large amounts and return exactly the same amount to pay 0 fees?
Does this violate the intended economic model of the protocol?
validation_requirements: Execute a flash loan returning exactly the borrowed amount
Verify that mtFee is 0 and transaction succeeds

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker borrows 1000 WETH via flashLoan, uses it for arbitrage, and returns exactly 1000 WETH. The pool registers baseBalance == _BASE_RESERVE_ and charges 0 fees, depriving the protocol of revenue.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of flash loan fee revenue for maintainers and LPs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to enforce fees

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for flash loan returning exact borrowed amounts

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as flash loans with 0 fee transfers

threats.title: Target-Reserve Desync via Asymmetric Rounding in sellShares
threats.hypothesis_description: sellShares reduces targets using divCeil (rounding up) but calculates user output using floor division. This causes targets to shrink faster than reserves over many transactions.
threats.investigation_rationale: Asymmetric rounding in AMMs can lead to systematic drift. Here, targets drift downwards relative to reserves, artificially pushing the pool into a BELOW_ONE state and altering the pricing curve.

proof_of_signal.pattern: divCeil for targets, floor for amounts

preconditions: shareAmount is small enough to cause rounding differences

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Targets decrease by ceil, reserves decrease by floor

analysis_questions: How many transactions does it take to meaningfully desync the targets?
Can an attacker profit from the resulting price curve shift?
validation_requirements: Simulate 10,000 small sellShares operations
Measure the drift between _BASE_TARGET_ and _BASE_RESERVE_

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker repeatedly mints and burns small amounts of shares. The asymmetric rounding causes the pool's targets to drop significantly below reserves, artificially lowering the token price for the attacker to buy.
prerequisites: Low gas fees to make repeated transactions viable

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Value leakage from LPs due to manipulated pricing curve
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: correctRState cannot fix this if reserves > targets

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing fuzz tests for cumulative rounding errors over many mint/burn cycles

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Cost of repeated mint/burn vs extracted value
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as high frequency of small mint/burn events

threats.title: Smart Contract Composability Broken by tx.origin in Factory Salt Computation
threats.hypothesis_description: Factory.create uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender to compute the CREATE2 salt and register the pool creator, breaking integrations with smart contract wallets and DAOs.
threats.investigation_rationale: Using tx.origin means a DAO executing a proposal to create a pool will register the executor's EOA as the creator. If the same EOA executes another proposal for the same pair, the salt collides and deployment fails.
preconditions: Pool created via a smart contract (e.g., Router, DAO, Multisig)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Use of tx.origin for identity attribution
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Smart contracts cannot own pools in userPools mapping

analysis_questions: Does this prevent the Router from properly attributing pool creation?
Will this cause CREATE2 collisions for active EOAs?
validation_requirements: Deploy two pools with identical parameters via a smart contract using the same EOA
Verify the second deployment reverts due to salt collision

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A multisig wallet attempts to deploy multiple pools for the same token pair (with different parameters). Because the same EOA submits the transactions, the salt collides and subsequent deployments fail.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: None directly, but severe functional limitation

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for pool creation via intermediary contracts

threats.title: Permanent Stranding of Reward Tokens due to Reward Rate Truncation
threats.hypothesis_description: In notifyRewardAmount, rewardRate is calculated via integer division. The remainder is permanently stranded in the contract and cannot be claimed by users.
threats.investigation_rationale: If amount is not perfectly divisible by _remainingRewardTime, the truncated dust remains in the contract forever. Over many epochs, this accumulates to a measurable loss of reward tokens.
preconditions: amount is not perfectly divisible by _remainingRewardTime

analysis_questions: How much dust accumulates over a year of weekly reward notifications?
Is there any mechanism to recover this dust?
validation_requirements: Simulate notifyRewardAmount with indivisible amounts
Calculate total claimed rewards vs notified rewards at epoch end

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not an attack, but a systematic value leak. The operator notifies 1000 tokens over 600 seconds. The rate is 1 token/sec. Only 600 tokens are distributed; 400 are permanently locked.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Slow accumulation of permanently locked reward tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin can use recover() to extract the dust

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing exact balance accounting tests post-epoch

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as growing reward token balance in contract

threats.title: Fee Rate Model Bypass via tx.origin in Swap Functions
threats.hypothesis_description: MagicLP uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender to query the fee rate model. Smart contracts integrating the pool cannot receive contract-specific fee tiers.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the protocol intends to offer volume discounts to specific aggregator contracts, it will fail because the fee model only sees the EOA that initiated the transaction.
preconditions: FeeRateModel applies different rates based on caller address
Swap is routed through a smart contract

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Intermediary contracts cannot be priced accurately

analysis_questions: Does the current or planned FeeRateModel rely on msg.sender?
Can a malicious user route through a privileged EOA to steal their discount?
validation_requirements: Deploy a FeeRateModel with a discount for a specific contract
Verify the contract does not receive the discount when called by a normal EOA

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A DEX aggregator is whitelisted for a 0% fee. A user trades through the aggregator, but because tx.origin is the user's EOA, the pool charges the standard 0.3% fee, breaking the aggregator's routing logic.
threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for fee application via intermediary contracts

threats.title: Admin Privilege Abuse: Complete Pool Drain via setParameters
threats.hypothesis_description: The setParameters function allows the implementation owner to arbitrarily extract base and quote tokens from the pool up to the minReserve limits.
threats.investigation_rationale: While minReserve limits exist, the admin can set them to 0 and extract the entire pool balance. This is a direct rug pull vector built into the core contract.

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Timelock or strict limits on extraction amounts

analysis_questions: Is the implementation owner a multisig or a single EOA?
Is there any off-chain monitoring for this function?
validation_requirements: Call setParameters with baseOutAmount = balance - 1
Verify funds are transferred to assetTo

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised admin key calls setParameters, setting baseOutAmount and quoteOutAmount to the pool's entire balance, instantly stealing all LP funds.
threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing security invariant tests for admin extraction limits

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Massive token transfers to unknown addresses

threats.title: Admin Privilege Abuse: Draining Reward Tokens via recover Function
threats.hypothesis_description: The recover function in LockingMultiRewards protects the staking token but lacks any protection for reward tokens, allowing the owner to steal all user rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function explicitly checks if the token is the stakingToken to prevent skimming staked funds, but allows arbitrary withdrawal of any other token, including active reward tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: tokenAddress.safeTransfer(owner, tokenAmount) with no check for rewardTokens

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Reward tokens can be drained, causing insolvency
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Check if tokenAddress is in rewardTokens array

analysis_questions: Does the protocol intend for the owner to be able to withdraw active reward tokens?
What happens to users when they try to claim rewards after a drain?
validation_requirements: Call recover on an active reward token
Verify users cannot claim rewards due to insufficient balance

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised admin calls recover() on the ARB reward token, draining the contract. When users' locks expire, their getRewards() calls revert because the contract lacks the ARB to pay them.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of all pending user rewards

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test ensuring active reward tokens cannot be recovered

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Large reward token transfers to owner

threats.title: Denial of Service for Large Trades on Extreme Price Pools due to Quadratic Overflow
threats.hypothesis_description: The _SolveQuadraticFunctionForTrade function calculates bAbs * bAbs, which can overflow uint256 for very large trade amounts on pools with extreme price ratios (high I parameter).
threats.investigation_rationale: If I is near its maximum of 10^36 and a user trades a large amount (e.g., 10^24 wei), the intermediate calculation bAbs * bAbs exceeds the maximum value of uint256, causing a revert.
preconditions: Pool has a very high I parameter (e.g., > 10^27)
User attempts a very large trade (e.g., > 10^24 wei)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unchecked multiplication of large intermediate values
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Use of 512-bit math or scaling down before multiplication

analysis_questions: What are the exact bounds of I and delta that trigger the overflow?
Are there realistic token pairs that would require such high I values?
validation_requirements: Write a test calling _SolveQuadraticFunctionForTrade with I=10^36 and delta=10^24
Verify it reverts with panic 0x11

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user tries to swap a large amount of a low-value token (like SHIB) in a pool with a high-value token (like WBTC). The transaction reverts due to overflow, preventing the trade.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of trading volume and fees
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Trade must be split into smaller chunks

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing fuzz tests with extreme upper bounds for I and delta

threats.title: Operator Privilege Abuse: User Lock Slot Exhaustion via stakeFor
threats.hypothesis_description: The stakeFor function allows an operator to create locks for any user. A malicious operator can fill a user's maxLocks slots with dust amounts, preventing the user from creating their own locks.
threats.investigation_rationale: Because maxLocks is strictly enforced (e.g., 13), an operator can call stakeFor 13 times across different epochs for a victim, consuming all their lock slots.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Operator can call _stakeFor for any account with lock_=true

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Third-party consumption of limited user resources
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: User opt-in for third-party locking or separate operator lock accounting

analysis_questions: Can the operator bypass the minLockAmount check?
How much capital does it cost the operator to grief a user?
validation_requirements: Call stakeFor as operator 13 times for a victim
Verify victim cannot call lock() themselves

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised operator bot calls stakeFor on high-value users, locking the minimum required amount 13 times. The users are now unable to lock their own large balances, losing out on the boost multiplier.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of potential yield for targeted users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Users must wait for locks to expire

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for operator griefing of user lock limits

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as many small stakeFor calls by operator
